985	\begin{lemma}
986	[Abelian-group exactness criterion for the section {\v C}ech complex]
987	  \label{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
988	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechAbExact,
989	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_isZero_homology_of_objD_exact,
990	    AlgebraicGeometry.ab_hom_finsetSum_apply,
991	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionToModuleAddEquiv,
992	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionToModuleAddEquiv_apply,
993	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionProdEquiv_symm_apply}
994	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
995	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_coface_match,
996	    lem:section_cech_module_exact}
997	  For \(1 \leq p\) the homology of the section {\v C}ech complex
998	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
999	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} vanishes,
1000	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1001	  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\). The argument splits into a homological
1002	  precursor and an unconditional ladder transport.
1003	  \begin{itemize}
1004	    \item The precursor \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_isZero\_homology\_of\_objD\_exact}\)
1005	      is pure homological algebra: \emph{given}
1006	      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying group homomorphisms of two
1007	      consecutive coface differentials \(\operatorname{objD} q\),
1008	      \(\operatorname{objD}(q+1)\), the homology of the section complex at degree
1009	      \(q+1\) is zero. It chains
1010	      \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\),
1011	      \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.exactAt\_iff'}\) (to expose the short complex
1012	      around the node), and the abelian-group exactness criterion
1013	      \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff\_function\_exact}\) (which states that
1014	      a short complex of abelian groups is exact iff for every \(x_2\) with
1015	      \(g(x_2) = 0\) there is \(x_1\) with \(f(x_1) = x_2\)).
1016	    \item The unconditional \(\operatorname{sectionCechAbExact}\) discharges that
1017	      hypothesis by transporting the already-proved \(R\)-module exactness
1018	      \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
1019	      Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the degreewise additive
1020	      isomorphism, using the ladder-transport lemma
1021	      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact.of\_ladder\_addEquiv\_of\_exact}\).
1022	  \end{itemize}
1023	\end{lemma}
1024	
1025	\begin{proof}
1026	  \uses{lem:section_cech_objd_apply, lem:section_cech_product_equiv,
1027	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1028	  Fix \(p = q + 1 \geq 1\). By the precursor
1029	  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_isZero\_homology\_of\_objD\_exact}\) it suffices to
1030	  exhibit \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying group homomorphisms of the
1031	  two consecutive coface differentials \(\operatorname{objD} q\),
1032	  \(\operatorname{objD}(q+1)\) of the section complex.
1033	
1034	  This exactness is obtained by the ladder transport
1035	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact.of\_ladder\_addEquiv\_of\_exact}\). The vertical maps
1036	  of the ladder are the degreewise additive isomorphisms
1037	  \(e_p = \prod_\sigma \varphi_\sigma \circ \operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_p\):
1038	  the product equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv} followed by the
1039	  coordinatewise comparison \(\varphi_\sigma\) identifying each section group with the
1040	  localised module \(M_{s_\sigma}\). This composite vertical map is the additive
1041	  equivalence \(\operatorname{sectionToModuleAddEquiv}\), whose \(\sigma\)-component is
1042	  \(\operatorname{sectionToModuleAddEquiv\_apply}\) (the forward coordinate formula) and
1043	  whose inverse-direction coordinate is \(\operatorname{sectionProdEquiv\_symm\_apply}\). The two commuting squares of the ladder are the
1044	  coface match: the abstract unfold of the differential as an alternating sum of face
1045	  restrictions (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}, the decl
1046	  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_objD\_apply}\)) together with the per-coordinate
1047	  identification of each face restriction with the localisation coface
1048	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}) shows
1049	  \(e_{p+1} \circ \operatorname{objD} p = \operatorname{dDiff}^{\,p} \circ e_p\). The
1050	  horizontal exactness fed into the transport is the \(R\)-module exactness
1051	  \(H = \operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
1052	  Transporting \(H\) across the additive ladder yields the desired
1053	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the \(\operatorname{objD}\) maps, discharging the
1054	  hypothesis of the precursor.
1055	\end{proof}

1084	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\)

1086	  R}\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(s : \iota \to R\) (\(\iota\) finite) a spanning
1087	  family of the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) =
1088	  \top\) (Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}). The \emph{un-localised section
1089	  {\v C}ech module complex} \(D^\bullet\) is the cochain complex of \(R\)-modules
1090	  whose degree-\(p\) term is the product over multi-indices
1091	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) of the away localisations
1092	  \[
1093	    D^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} M_{s_\sigma},
1094	    \qquad s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
1095	    \qquad M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}
1096	      \bigl(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\bigr)\, M,
1097	  \]
1098	  with differential \(d^p : D^p \to D^{p+1}\) the alternating sum
1099	  \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j\, \delta^j\) of the coface comparison maps \(\delta^j\), each of

1100	  which is the canonical away-localisation comparison
1101	  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) sending a fraction to its image under
1102	  the further localisation at the omitted factor. The single-coefficient comparison
1103	  map and its localisation property are recorded by the \(\mathtt{dCoface}\) and
1104	  \(\mathtt{dCoeff}\) data and the differential by \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) (with action
1105	  \(\mathtt{dDiff\_apply}\)); the auxiliary \(\mathtt{spanIdx}\)/\(\mathtt{spanIdx\_spec}\)
1106	  select a witnessing index of the spanning relation.

1109	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and to its localisations by three
1110	  comparison families. First, the maps \(\mathtt{dToCech}\) identify each term

1113	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} as a localised module
1114	  (\(\mathtt{dToCech\_isLocalizedModule}\)), intertwining the differentials
1115	  (\(\mathtt{cechCoface\_dToCech}\), \(\mathtt{dToCech\_comm}\)); the section-side
1116	  cofaces are \(\mathtt{cechCofaceLin}\) with action \(\mathtt{cechCoface\_apply}\).
1117	  Second, localising \(D^\bullet\) at a spanning element \(s_r\) reproduces the
1118	  \emph{localised} {\v C}ech complex: the localised differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1119	  (action \(\mathtt{locDiff\_apply}\)) agrees, via
1120	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\), with the dependent-coefficient combinatorial
1121	  differential, and its exactness is recorded as \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\). The
1122	  localisation comparison maps \(\mathtt{fLoc}\) (action \(\mathtt{fLoc\_apply}\))
1123	  exhibit each localised term as a localised module
1124	  (\(\mathtt{fLoc\_isLocalizedModule}\)) intertwining \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) with
1125	  \(\mathtt{locDiff}\) (\(\mathtt{locDiff\_fLoc}\), \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\)).

1127	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\): localising the away module \(M_{s_\sigma}\)
1128	  further at \(s_r\) gives \(M_{s_r s_\sigma}\). This is the keystone
1129	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\) (the composite of two away
1130	  localisations is again the away localisation at the product), with the cancellation
1131	  bookkeeping \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.Inverts.smul\_pow\_cancel}\). Thus localising

1133	  the localised Čech complex of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, and
1134	  exactness of \(D^\bullet\) can be checked one localisation at a time.
1135	\end{definition}

1137	\begin{lemma}
1138	
1139	\leanok
1140	[Positive-degree exactness of the section {\v C}ech module complex]
1141	  \label{lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1142	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dDiff_exact}
1143	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1144	  With the notation of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}, the
1145	  un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\) is exact in positive
1146	  degrees: for every \(p\),
1147	  \[
1148	    \operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(d^{p+1} : D^{p+1} \to D^{p+2},\;
1149	      d^{p+2} : D^{p+2} \to D^{p+3}\bigr).
1150	  \]
1151	  This is step~(a) of the affine vanishing: the node-by-node exactness of the
1152	  un-localised module complex, from which the homology vanishing of the section
1153	  {\v C}ech complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, step~(c)) is read off.
1154	\end{lemma}
1155	
1156	\begin{proof}
1157	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1158	  \leanok
1159	  Exactness of \(D^\bullet\) at each positive-degree node is a local-to-global
1160	  statement over the spanning family. By the local-to-global exactness criterion
1161	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\) (with the
1162	  spanning hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\)), it
1163	  suffices to verify \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p+1}, d^{p+2})\) after
1164	  localising at each spanning element \(s_r\). In the localisation at \(s_r\) the
1165	  comparison family \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\) identifies the localised
1166	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) with the localised Čech differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1167	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} (using the localisation
1168	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\) of
1169	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\)). Exactness of that
1170	  localised differential is \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\), which is in turn the
1171	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial exactness transported along
1172	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\); the contracting homotopy at the fixed index
1173	  \(r\) (where \(s_r\) is invertible) furnishes \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\),
1174	  giving the localised exactness one localisation at a time. Reassembling over the
1175	  spanning family yields the un-localised exactness.
1176	\end{proof}

1286	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)) = M_{s_\sigma}\); under these identifications each face

1312	  \paragraph{Node-by-node exactness via local-to-global and the dependent port.}

1314	  \((s, hs)\). The local-to-global exactness criterion
1315	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\, hs\)

1345	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1346	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1347	
1348	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1349	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1350	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1351	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1352	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1353	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1354	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1355	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1356	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1357	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1358	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1359	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1360	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1361	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1362	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1363	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1364	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1365	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1366	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1367	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1368	\(\delta\)-functor identification.

1408	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1409	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1410	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1411	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1412	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1413	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1414	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1415	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1416	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1417	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1418	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1419	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1420	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1421	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1422	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1423	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1424	  \[
1425	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1426	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1427	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1428	  \]
1429	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1430	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1431	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1432	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1433	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1434	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1435	  \[
1436	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1437	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1438	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1439	  \]
1440	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1441	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1442	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1443	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1444	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1445	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1446	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1447	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1448	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1449	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1450	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1451	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1452	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1453	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1454	
1455	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1456	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1457	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1458	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1459	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1460	\end{definition}

1542	  % L1163--1196).
1543	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Then there is an isomorphism
1544	  % $$
1545	  % \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{F})
1546	  % =
1547	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1548	  % $$
1549	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F} \in \Ob(\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X))$."
1550	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1551	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}.}
1552	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1553	  open covering. For every presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\)
1554	  there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes of \emph{abelian groups}
1555	  \[
1556	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1557	      \;=\;
1558	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}),
1559	  \]
1560	  natural in \(\mathcal{F} \in \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). Here the left side is
1561	  the cochain complex obtained by applying the contravariant
1562	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{F})\) to the chain complex
1563	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1564	  and the right side is the section {\v C}ech complex of
1565	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}.
1566	\end{lemma}
1567	
1568	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We saw in the discussion just above the lemma that
1569	% $$
1570	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(
1571	% (j_{i_0\ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_p}},
1572	% \mathcal{F})
1573	% =
1574	% \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1575	% $$
1576	% Hence we see that it is indeed the case that the direct sum
1577	% $$
1578	% \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
1579	% (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}
1580	% $$
1581	% represents the functor
1582	% $$
1583	% \mathcal{F}
1584	% \longmapsto
1585	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0\ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1586	% $$
1587	% Hence by Categories, Yoneda Lemma \ref{categories-lemma-yoneda}
1588	% we see that there is a complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ with terms
1589	% as given. It is a simple matter to see that the maps are as given
1590	% in the lemma."
1591	\begin{proof}
1592	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1593	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1594	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1595	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1596	  \[
1597	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1598	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1599	    \bigr)
1600	    \;\cong\;
1601	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1602	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1603	    \;\cong\;
1604	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1605	  \]
1606	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1607	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1608	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1609	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1610	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1611	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1612	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1613	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1614	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1615	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1616	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1617	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1618	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1619	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1620	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1621	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1622	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1623	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1624	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1625	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1626	\end{proof}

1651	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
1652	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1653	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :
1654	  X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\) is the image presheaf of the augmentation
1655	  \[
1656	    \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{1},
1657	  \]
1658	  where the target \(\mathbf{1} = \operatorname{PresheafOfModules.unit}(
1659	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
1660	  regarded as a presheaf of modules over itself. Concretely
1661	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when \(W\) is contained in some
1662	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term
1663	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 = \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i)\) of the free
1664	  presheaf complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} carries the
1665	  augmentation map \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) onto this image
1666	  presheaf.
1667	\end{definition}

1669	\begin{lemma}
1670	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1671	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1673	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1674	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1675	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1676	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1677	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1678	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1679	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1680	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1681	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1682	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1683	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1684	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1685	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1686	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1687	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1688	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1689	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1690	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1691	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1692	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1693	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1694	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1695	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1696	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1697	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1698	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1699	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1700	\end{lemma}
1701	
1702	\begin{proof}
1703	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1704	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1705	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1706	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1707	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1708	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1709	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1710	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1711	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1712	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1713	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1714	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1715	  quasi-isomorphism.
1716	\end{proof}

1718	\begin{lemma}
1719	
1720	[Sectionwise description of the evaluated free complex]
1721	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
1722	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_X,
1723	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le,
1724	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_isZero_of_not_le}
1725	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1726	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1727	  % (proof, the sectionwise description of $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$) (read from
1728	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1227--1252).
1729	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
1730	  % if $i \in I_1$. ... If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
1731	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$ and
1732	  % $K^{ext}_p(W) = \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)$.
1733	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1734	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$."
1735	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1736	  (description of the evaluated complex).}
1737	  Fix an open \(V \subset X\) and write \(I_1 = I_1(V) = \{\,i \in I : V \leq U_i\,\}\)
1738	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex
1739	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} at
1740	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1741	  \[
1742	    K(\mathcal{U})_p(V)
1743	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1744	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1745	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}
1746	        \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1747	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
1748	  \]
1749	  because \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1750	  \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise (poset hom-set is a subsingleton), and
1751	  \(V \leq U_\sigma = U_{\sigma(0)} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\sigma(p)}\) holds iff
1752	  \(\sigma\) takes all its values in \(I_1\). Under this identification the
1753	  differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) is the alternating-sum combinatorial
1754	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1755	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated
1756	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1757	  \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\) when
1758	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1759	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1760	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\)) is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1761	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1762	  The Lean entry point for the degree-\(p\) unfolding is
1763	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) (which exposes
1764	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p = \coprod_\sigma \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)\) by
1765	  \(\operatorname{rfl}\)) composed with
1766	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\) at \(V\). The full degreewise
1767	  identification is assembled from three pieces: \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\)
1768	  (evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the coproduct, giving
1769	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_\sigma
1770	  (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\)),
1771	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\) (a surviving summand with
1772	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\)), and
1773	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\) (a summand with
1774	  \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) is the zero object); collapsing the coproduct over the
1775	  surviving \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) gives
1776	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1777	\end{lemma}
1778	
1779	\begin{proof}
1780	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1781	  The degreewise identity is the value at \(V\) of the explicit description of the
1782	  free presheaf \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)\) recorded in
1783	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free
1784	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-module on \(\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)\), which is
1785	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) if \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise. Applying
1786	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\;V\) to the coproduct decomposition
1787	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) commutes with the direct sum and
1788	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether
1789	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\), i.e.\ whether \(\sigma\) factors through \(I_1\); the surviving
1790	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1791	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1792	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1793	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1794	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1795	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in
1796	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_structure_presheaf}.
1797	\end{proof}

1823	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
1824	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
1825	  % $$

1827	  % Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
1828	  % $$
1829	  % h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)
1830	  % $$
1831	  % by the rule
1832	  % $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} =
1833	  % \left\{ \begin{matrix}

1836	  % \end{matrix} \right.$"
1837	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1838	  (differential and contracting homotopy of the evaluated complex).}
1839	  Fix a nonempty index set \(J\) (in the application \(J = I_1(V)\)), a chosen index
1840	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in J\), and a single coefficient module \(M\). The
1841	  \emph{constant-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech engine} consists of: the
1842	  alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\) on the
1843	  full simplex over \(J\) with coefficients \(M\),
1844	  \[
1845	    (\operatorname{combDifferential} t)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1846	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\, t_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}},
1847	  \]
1848	  which squares to zero, \(\operatorname{combDifferential} \circ
1849	  \operatorname{combDifferential} = 0\) (\(\operatorname{combDifferential\_comp}\)); the
1850	  \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} homotopy \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\),
1851	  \((\operatorname{combHomotopy}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, u)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1852	  = u_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}}\) when \(i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) and \(0\) otherwise; and
1853	  the contracting identity \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\)
1854	  (\(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_spec}\)), which yields positive-degree exactness
1855	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_exact}\) and the cocycle-splitting form
1856	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_eq\_of\_cocycle}\). The remaining declarations are
1857	  sign- and index-bookkeeping consumed by the contracting identity: the
1858	  succ-shift reindexing \(\operatorname{cons\_comp\_succAbove\_succ}\) and the
1859	  alternating-sign flip \(\operatorname{combSign\_flip}\), together with the
1860	  degenerate-case simplification \(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_zero}\). The generic
1861	  category-theory helper \(\operatorname{isZero\_sigma\_of\_forall\_isZero}\) (a
1862	  coproduct of zero objects is a zero object) is recorded here as well, used to collapse
1863	  the empty-index direct sums of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}. This engine is
1864	  self-contained combinatorics on \(\operatorname{Fin.cons}\),

1866	  \(\operatorname{Finset.sum\_involution}\); it mirrors the contracting homotopy of the
1867	  Stacks \texttt{lemma-homology-complex} proof.
1868	\end{lemma}

1902	  % K^{ext}_p(W) =
1903	  % \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W).
1904	  % $$
1905	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1906	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$.
1907	  % Moreover, the differential of the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$
1908	  % is given by
1909	  % $$
1910	  % d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} =
1911	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
1912	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
1913	  % $$"
1914	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1915	  (the evaluated complex \(K^{ext}_\bullet(W)\) and its differential).}
1916	  Fix an open \(V\) with \(I_1(V) = \{\, i : V \leq U_i \,\} \neq \varnothing\), and
1917	  let \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) be the single coefficient module
1918	  (\(\operatorname{coverSectionModule}\)). The \emph{engine chain complex}
1919	  \(C_\bullet = \operatorname{cechEngineComplex}\, \mathcal{U}\, V\) is the chain
1920	  complex of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-modules with
1921	  \[
1922	    C_p \;=\; \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1923	    \qquad (\operatorname{cechEngineX}),
1924	  \]
1925	  the coproduct taken in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(\mathcal{O}_X(V))\), and with
1926	  differential \(d = \operatorname{cechEngineD}\) given on the injection
1927	  \(\iota_\sigma\) of the index \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)\) by the
1928	  alternating sum of index-drop reindexings,
1929	  \[
1930	    d \circ \iota_\sigma
1931	      \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{p} (-1)^i\, \iota_{\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i}
1932	    \qquad (\operatorname{cechEngineD\_\iota}),
1933	  \]
1934	  i.e.\ the chain transpose of the index-\emph{raise} differential
1935	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) of
1936	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}. Then:
1937	  \begin{itemize}
1938	    \item \(d \circ d = 0\) (\(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_comp}\)), so the family
1939	      assembles into the chain complex \(C_\bullet = \operatorname{ChainComplex.of}\)
1940	      (\(\operatorname{cechEngineComplex}\)).
1941	    \item The \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} map
1942	      \(s \mapsto \iota_{\operatorname{Fin.cons}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, \sigma}\)
1943	      (\(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend}\), with its injection computation
1944	      \(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend\_\iota}\)) is a contracting homotopy in
1945	      positive degree: \(s \circ d + d \circ s = \mathrm{id}\)
1946	      (\(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend\_spec}\)).
1947	    \item Hence \(C_\bullet\) is exact in positive degree,
1948	      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(\operatorname{cechEngineD}(n+1),
1949	      \operatorname{cechEngineD} n)\) (\(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_exact}\)).
1950	  \end{itemize}
1951	  Four object-half bricks realise the degreewise identification
1952	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong C_p\) consumed by
1953	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso}: evaluation commutes with the coproduct;
1954	  the non-surviving summands (those \(\sigma\) with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\)) drop to the
1955	  zero object (\(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
1956	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalEngine\_X}\)); and the surviving index type is reindexed
1957	  by the equivalence
1958	  \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv} : (\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)) \simeq
1959	  \{\, \sigma \;//\; V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma \,\}\),
1960	  where the membership criterion
1961	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma
1962	  \iff \forall k,\ V \leq U_{\sigma(k)}\) is
1963	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\).
1964	\end{lemma}
1965	
1966	\begin{proof}
1967	  \uses{lem:free_cech_engine}
1968	  The complex \(C_\bullet\) is the chain/coproduct dual of the constant-coefficient
1969	  core of Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}. The differential
1970	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) is defined on coproduct injections by the
1971	  alternating-sign index-drop \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove}
1972	  i\); both \(d \circ d = 0\) and the contracting identity
1973	  \(s \circ d + d \circ s = \mathrm{id}\) are proved by extending along the
1974	  injections with \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) and reducing each
1975	  injection-component to the corresponding combinatorial cancellation of
1976	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential\_comp}\) respectively
1977	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy\_spec}\) (the prepend
1978	  \(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend}\) is the coproduct dual of
1979	  \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\)). Positive-degree exactness
1980	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_exact}\) reads off the contracting identity. The four
1981	  object-half bricks are immediate: evaluation preserves \(\coprod\) and the surviving
1982	  summands are picked out by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) under the criterion
1983	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\), the rest being zero objects
1984	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\)).
1985	\end{proof}

2003	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2004	  % (proof, the description of $K^{ext}_p(W)$ and its differential) (read from

2006	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
2007	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$
2008	  % and
2009	  % $$
2010	  % K^{ext}_p(W) =

2012	  % $$

2014	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$.
2015	  % Moreover, the differential of the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$
2016	  % is given by
2017	  % $$
2018	  % d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} =
2019	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
2020	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
2021	  % $$"
2022	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2023	  (the evaluated complex and its differential).}
2024	  Fix an open \(V\) with \(I_1(V) = \{\, i : V \leq U_i \,\} \neq \varnothing\). There
2025	  is an isomorphism of chain complexes of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-modules
2026	  \[
2027	    \bigl((\operatorname{eval} V).\operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\bigr)
2028	      \bigl(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex} \mathcal{U}\bigr)
2029	    \;\cong\;
2030	    C_\bullet,
2031	    \qquad
2032	    C_p = \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
2033	  \]
2034	  where the coproduct is taken in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(\mathcal{O}_X(V))\) and the
2035	  differential of \(C_\bullet\) is the constant-coefficient {\v C}ech differential
2036	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) on the index set \(J = I_1(V)\)
2037	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}). The isomorphism is \emph{degreewise} and
2038	  \emph{intertwines the evaluated alternating-face differential with}
2039	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\). It is the chain/coproduct dual of the section-side
2040	  cochain identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}; isolating it
2041	  here gives the empty and nonempty cases (Lemmas~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}
2042	  and~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_nonempty}) a common, ready degreewise model.
2043	\end{lemma}
2044	
2045	\begin{proof}
2046	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
2047	  \emph{Degreewise.} By \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) of
2048	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the
2049	  coproduct,
2050	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_{\sigma :
2051	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0} (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}
2052	  (\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\). Split the index set into the \(\sigma\) landing in
2053	  \(I_1(V)\) and the rest. Each summand with \(\sigma\) valued in \(I_1(V)\) (so
2054	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\)) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) by
2055	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); each remaining summand is the zero
2056	  object by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\). Hence the coproduct
2057	  collapses to \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V) =
2058	  C_p\).
2059	
2060	  \emph{Differential match.} The evaluated differential is \((\operatorname{eval} V)\)
2061	  applied to the alternating face sum
2062	  \(\operatorname{AlternatingFaceMapComplex.objD}(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}
2063	  \mathcal{U})\). The face \(\delta_i\) reindexes the \(\sigma\)-summand by
2064	  \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (the face action of
2065	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}\)); restricted to the
2066	  \(I_1\)-summands this is exactly the index-drop that the engine encodes, and the sign
2067	  \((-1)^i\) is supplied by \(\operatorname{objD}\). The match is verified on the
2068	  coproduct injections by \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\), reusing the
2069	  naturality of \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\); summing with signs
2070	  identifies the evaluated differential with
2071	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\). This is the hard content of the
2072	  identification and the chain/coproduct dual of the cochain coface match in
2073	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}.
2074	
2075	  \emph{The three layers commuted past.} The degreewise iso is the \(3\)-fold
2076	  composite
2077	  \[
2078	    (\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p)
2079	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\;}
2080	      \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0}
2081	        (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))
2082	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\;}
2083	      \coprod_{V \leq U_\sigma} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
2084	      \xrightarrow{\;(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \ldots)^{-1}\;}
2085	      C_p,
2086	  \]
2087	  whose layers are: (1) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) = the comparison
2088	  \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\) expressing that evaluation at
2089	  \(V\) commutes with the coproduct \(\coprod\); (2) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
2090	  which kills the summands with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) (these evaluate to zero objects
2091	  by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\)); (3)
2092	  \((\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \operatorname{survivingEquiv}\
2093	  (\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\, \ldots))^{-1}\), which reindexes the
2094	  surviving summands by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) of
2095	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} and identifies each evaluated summand with the
2096	  single coefficient \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) via \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\).
2097	
2098	  \emph{Naturality past each layer.} The reindex \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) is
2099	  natural in the face map \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\):
2100	  a surviving index \(\sigma\) (i.e.\ \(V \leq U_\sigma\), equivalently
2101	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma\) by
2102	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\)) is carried to a surviving
2103	  \(\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (still \(V \leq U_{\sigma \circ
2104	  \operatorname{succAbove}\, i}\), since dropping a coordinate only weakens the meet),
2105	  so the drop-zeros layer (2) and the whisker layer (3) commute with every face. On a
2106	  surviving summand the evaluated transition \(\operatorname{freeYoneda}.\mathrm{map}
2107	  (\operatorname{homOfLE}\, \ldots)\) collapses to the identity under
2108	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); hence each face \(\delta_i\) matches the
2109	  engine reindexing \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\)
2110	  summand-for-summand, and summing with the \(\operatorname{objD}\) signs \((-1)^i\)
2111	  identifies the evaluated alternating-face differential with
2112	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) (\(= \) the chain transpose of
2113	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\)).
2114	
2115	  \emph{The genuine difficulty: variance.} The free \emph{chain} differential
2116	  \emph{lowers} \(\mathrm{Fin}\)-arity (\(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ
2117	  \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\), faces), whereas
2118	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) \emph{raises} it (it inserts an
2119	  index, summing over \(i \in I_1\)). The two are reconciled because on the finite
2120	  biproduct \(\coprod = \prod\) the insertion/deletion adjunction makes the index-drop
2121	  chain differential the transpose of the index-raise cochain differential; this
2122	  transpose is precisely what \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) of
2123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} packages, and verifying the commutation amounts to
2124	  the injectionwise check \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) outlined above.
2125	\end{proof}

2129	[Evaluated free complex over an open meeting no cover member]
2130	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}

2132	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_isZero_of_isEmpty,
2133	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverStructurePresheaf_eval_isZero_of_isEmpty,
2134	    AlgebraicGeometry.isZero_homology_of_isZero_X}
2135	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
2136	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2137	  % (proof, the empty case) (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex,
2138	  % L1228--1230).
2139	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $I_1 = \emptyset$, then the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W) = 0$
2140	  % so there is nothing to prove."
2141	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2142	  (empty case).}
2143	  With the notation of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}, suppose
2144	  \(I_1(V) = \varnothing\), i.e.\ \(V\) is contained in no cover member \(U_i\). Then
2145	  the evaluated augmented complex is identically zero: every degree-\(p\) term
2146	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) is the empty
2147	  direct sum \(0\) (there is no map \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \varnothing\)) and the
2148	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\). Consequently the
2149	  evaluation at \(V\) of \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\) is a morphism of zero
2150	  complexes, which is a quasi-isomorphism (both sides have vanishing homology). The Lean
2151	  bricks are \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_isZero\_of\_isEmpty}\) (each evaluated source
2152	  term is a zero object, via the coproduct-of-zeros helper
2153	  \(\operatorname{isZero\_sigma\_of\_forall\_isZero}\) of
2154	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}),
2155	  \(\operatorname{coverStructurePresheaf\_eval\_isZero\_of\_isEmpty}\) (the evaluated
2156	  augmentation target is zero), and the generic
2157	  \(\operatorname{isZero\_homology\_of\_isZero\_X}\) (a short complex with zero middle
2158	  term has vanishing homology), which together force every
2159	  \(\operatorname{homologyMap}\) to be a map of zero objects.
2160	\end{lemma}
2161	
2162	\begin{proof}
2163	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
2164	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} each evaluated term is
2165	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\); when
2166	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\) the index type \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) is empty, so the
2167	  term is the zero object, and likewise \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\). A
2168	  morphism whose source and target complexes are both zero (so all their homology
2169	  objects are zero, \(\operatorname{IsZero}\)) is a quasi-isomorphism; in Lean this is
2170	  immediate from \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\) since each
2171	  \(\operatorname{homologyMap}\) is a map of zero objects, hence an isomorphism.
2172	\end{proof}

2255	  % + (i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) d(s)_{i_1 \ldots i_p}$
2256	  % which is equal to $s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ as desired."

2258	  (the contracting identity).}

2260	  (\(I_1(V) \neq \varnothing\), \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in I_1\) fixed), the transported
2261	  prepend homotopy \(h\) satisfies the contracting identity
2262	  \[
2263	    d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}
2264	  \]
2265	  on the augmented evaluated complex
2266	  \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1(V) \to K(\mathcal{U})_0(V) \to
2267	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) \to 0\) (including the degree-\(0\)/degree-\((-1)\)

2272	\begin{proof}
2273	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy, lem:free_cech_engine}
2274	  This is the same alternating-sum cancellation as
2275	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy\_spec}\)
2276	  (\(\operatorname{combDifferential}(\operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, t) +
2277	  \operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, (\operatorname{combDifferential} t) = t\)),
2278	  specialised to coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Expanding
2279	  \((dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\): the \(j = 0\) term of \(dh\) prepends and then
2280	  removes \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\), returning \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\); every remaining term
2281	  of \(dh\) cancels in pairs against the corresponding term of \(hd\) (the
2282	  prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) of the differential), because removing an index
2283	  \(j \geq 1\) after prepending \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) equals prepending
2284	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) after removing index \(j-1\), with opposite signs. The net
2285	  result is \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), i.e.\ \(dh + hd = \mathrm{id}\); the index
2286	  bookkeeping is the \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.cons\_comp\_succAbove\_succ}\) /
2287	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combSign\_flip}\) content of
2288	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}.

2444	\begin{lemma}[Right adjoint of a mono-preserving left adjoint preserves injectives]

2450	  Let \(L \dashv R\) be an adjunction between abelian (or, more generally, suitable)
2451	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every

2541	\begin{lemma}
2542	[Hom into an injective preserves exactness and quasi-isomorphisms]
2543	  \label{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}
2544	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.preadditiveYoneda_obj_preservesFiniteColimits_of_injective,
2545	    AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_map_preadditiveYoneda_of_injective}
2546	  \uses{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2547	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category and \(I\) an injective object. Then the
2548	  contravariant hom functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I) : \mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}} \to
2549	  \mathrm{Ab}\) is exact: it is automatically left exact (preserves finite limits), and
2550	  injectivity of \(I\) makes it carry epimorphisms to epimorphisms, so it preserves
2551	  finite colimits and hence preserves homology. Consequently \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I)\)
2552	  carries quasi-isomorphisms of chain complexes in \(\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}}\) to
2553	  quasi-isomorphisms of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-cochain complexes. Both statements
2554	  are for a general abelian category, the right generality for reuse in
2555	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}. The mechanism --- a right adjoint / exact
2556	  contravariant functor preserves the relevant exactness --- is the same homological
2557	  principle behind Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint}.
2558	\end{lemma}
2559	
2560	\begin{proof}
2561	  \uses{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2562	  For injective \(I\), injectivity gives that \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I)\) carries
2563	  epimorphisms to epimorphisms. Combined with the automatic preservation of finite limits
2564	  (left exactness), the short-exact-sequence criterion shows it preserves finite colimits;
2565	  hence, being also left exact, it preserves homology. A functor that preserves homology
2566	  sends quasi-isomorphisms of chain complexes to quasi-isomorphisms degreewise, which is
2567	  the second statement.
2568	\end{proof}

2599	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{U} : U =
2600	  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be any open covering, and let \(\mathcal{I}\) be an
2601	  \emph{injective} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the {\v C}ech cohomology of
2602	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is concentrated in degree zero:
2603	  \[
2604	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) =
2605	    \begin{cases}

2607	      0 & p > 0.

2609	  \]
2610	  The positive-degree vanishing holds over an \emph{arbitrary finite family of opens}
2611	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\), with no covering hypothesis: the augmented free {\v C}ech
2612	  resolution is exact \emph{stalkwise} — at a point \(x\) it is the augmented simplicial
2613	  chain complex of the full simplex on \(\{i \mid x \in U_i\}\) with coefficients
2614	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\), which is contractible — and a covering hypothesis only fixes the
2615	  identity of the augmentation target \(\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}\), never the exactness.
2616	  Consequently the lemma applies to any standard covering data of the affine cover system,
2617	  with no restriction to coverings of the whole space.

2631	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-map-into} we have
2632	% $$
2633	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{I})
2634	% =
2635	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}).
2636	% $$
2637	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-homology-complex} we have that $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ is
2638	% quasi-isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}[0]$. Hence by
2639	% the exactness of Hom into $\mathcal{I}$ mentioned above we see
2640	% that $\check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all
2641	% $i > 0$."
2642	\begin{proof}
2643	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_op_identification, lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso, lem:hom_into_injective_exact}
2644	  The argument has two independent parts.
2645	
2646	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
2647	  left adjoint to the inclusion
2648	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
2649	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
2650	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
2651	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
2652	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
2653	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
2654	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
2655	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
2656	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2657	
2658	  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
2659	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
2660	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
2661	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
2662	  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
2663	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2664	  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
2665	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
2666	  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
2667	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
2668	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
2669	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
2670	  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
2671	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
2672	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\).
2673	
2674	  \emph{Categorical assembly.} Concretely, the exactness is produced by the
2675	  opposite-transport bridge. The free {\v C}ech augmentation is a quasi-isomorphism
2676	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}); forming its opposite complex and
2677	  applying \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) keeps it a quasi-isomorphism, since
2678	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as a presheaf of modules (Part 1) and
2679	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) then preserves homology
2680	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}). Transporting source and target of this
2681	  opposite quasi-isomorphism across the opposite-transport isomorphisms
2682	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_op_identification} and
2683	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso}) identifies it with the augmentation
2684	  of the section {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
2685	  \mathcal{I})\) onto the degree-\(0\)-concentrated target \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\).
2686	  The latter has vanishing homology in positive degrees, so the section {\v C}ech complex
2687	  does too. Hence \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
2688	  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
2689	  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
2690	  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
2691	\end{proof}

2711	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1592--1628).
2712	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a ringed space.
2713	  % Let
2714	  % $$
2715	  % 0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H} \to 0
2716	  % $$
2717	  % be a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.

2723	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
2724	  \texttt{lemma-ses-cech-h1}.}
2725	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and let
2726	  \[
2727	    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H} \to 0
2728	  \]
2729	  be a short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Let \(U \subset X\) be
2730	  open. If there is a cofinal system of open coverings \(\mathcal{U}\) of \(U\) with
2731	  \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\), then the map on sections
2732	  \(\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)\) is surjective. Equivalently, the short exact
2733	  sequence is exact on sections over such \(U\).
2734	\end{lemma}
2735	
2736	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Take an element $s \in \mathcal{H}(U)$. Choose an open
2737	% covering
2738	% $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ such that
2739	% (a) $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ and (b)
2740	% $s|_{U_i}$ is the image of a section $s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)$.
2741	% Since we can certainly find $\mathcal{U}$ such that (b) holds
2742	% it follows from the assumptions of the lemma that we can find
2743	% $\mathcal{U}$ such that (a) and (b) both hold.
2744	% Consider the sections
2745	% $$
2746	% s_{i_0i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
2747	% $$
2748	% Since $s_i$ lifts $s$ we see that $s_{i_0i_1} \in \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0i_1})$.
2749	% By the vanishing of $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$ we can
2750	% find sections $t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$ such that
2751	% $$
2752	% s_{i_0i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
2753	% $$
2754	% Then clearly the sections $s_i - t_i$ satisfy the sheaf condition
2755	% and glue to a section of $\mathcal{G}$ over $U$ which maps to $s$.
2756	% Hence we win."
2757	\begin{proof}
2758	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
2759	  Let \(s \in \mathcal{H}(U)\). Choose an open covering \(\mathcal{U} : U =
2760	  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) fine enough that (a) \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},
2761	  \mathcal{F}) = 0\) and (b) each restriction \(s|_{U_i}\) lifts to a section
2762	  \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\); both can be arranged simultaneously because the
2763	  coverings with property (a) are cofinal and (b) holds on any sufficiently fine
2764	  cover (surjectivity of \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}\) is a local condition).
2765	  On the double overlaps form
2766	  \[
2767	    s_{i_0 i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}.
2768	  \]
2769	  Because the \(s_i\) all lift the single section \(s\), the difference
2770	  \(s_{i_0 i_1}\) maps to \(0\) in \(\mathcal{H}\), hence lies in the subsheaf
2771	  \(\mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 i_1})\); the family \((s_{i_0 i_1})\) is a {\v C}ech
2772	  \(1\)-cocycle for \(\mathcal{F}\). By \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)
2773	  it is a coboundary: there are \(t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)\) with
2774	  \(s_{i_0 i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}\). Then the
2775	  corrected sections \(s_i - t_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) agree on overlaps, so by the
2776	  sheaf condition they glue to a global section of \(\mathcal{G}\) over \(U\) whose
2777	  image in \(\mathcal{H}\) is \(s\). Hence \(\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)\) is
2778	  surjective.
2779	\end{proof}

2781	\section{Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object}
2782	
2783	The two vanishing lemmas below speak about the \emph{absolute} sheaf cohomology
2784	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module over an open \(U\). Their
2785	dimension-shift proof needs three structural facts about \(H^p(U, -)\): a degree-zero
2786	identification with global sections, vanishing on injective modules, and a long exact
2787	sequence attached to a short exact sequence of modules. We realize \(H^p(U, -)\) as the
2788	\emph{Ext of a corepresenting object} in the single abelian category
2789	\(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\): there is an
2790	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresenting the
2791	sections-over-\(U\) functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\), and we set
2792	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2793	\mathcal{F})\). The corepresenting object is built \emph{without} the extension-by-zero
2794	functor — it is the sheafification of the free module on the representable presheaf
2795	\(\mathbf{y}\,U\) — and the realization keeps the injective module in the \emph{second}
2796	Ext argument, where Mathlib supplies all three facts off the shelf with \emph{no} appeal
2797	to restriction of module sheaves. The corepresenting object and its corepresentability
2798	are fixed first, then the following definition fixes the realization, and the Mathlib
2799	dependency anchors after it pin the exact results it rests on.
2800	
2801	\begin{definition}
2802	
2803	\leanok
2804	[Extension-by-zero structure sheaf as a corepresenting object]
2805	  \label{def:jshriek_ou}
2806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU}
2807	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction,
2808	    lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2809	  \textit{Corepresenting object for sections over an open, built without the
2810	  extension-by-zero functor.}
2811	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subscheme, regarded through the
2812	  representable presheaf \(\mathbf{y}\,U\) on the site of opens of \(X\). The
2813	  \emph{extension-by-zero structure sheaf} \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) is the object of
2814	  \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) obtained by sheafifying the free
2815	  presheaf of modules on \(\mathbf{y}\,U\):
2816	  \[
2817	    j_!\mathcal{O}_U \;:=\; \operatorname{sheafification}\bigl(
2818	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)\bigr)
2819	    \;\in\; X.\mathrm{Modules},
2820	  \]
2821	  where \(\operatorname{free}\) is the free presheaf-of-modules functor on the
2822	  representable (the degree-zero generator of the free presheaf complex of
2823	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}) and
2824	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) is the sheafification adjunction of
2825	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}. Up to isomorphism this is the classical
2826	  extension-by-zero \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) of the structure sheaf of \(U\) along the open
2827	  immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\); crucially, we build only this \emph{object} as a
2828	  sheafified free module, never the extension-by-zero \emph{functor} \(j_!\). The object
2829	  exists unconditionally: the free presheaf of modules on a representable and the
2830	  sheafification of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are both supplied by Mathlib.
2831	\end{definition}
2832	
2833	\begin{lemma}
2834	
2835	[Corepresentability of global sections over \(U\)]
2836	  \label{lem:jshriek_corepr}
2837	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_homEquiv, AlgebraicGeometry.sheafificationHomAddEquiv}
2838	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2839	  \textit{The object \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the sections-over-\(U\) functor.}
2840	  For every \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) there is a natural additive
2841	  isomorphism
2842	  \[
2843	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
2844	    \;\cong\;
2845	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}) \;=\; \mathcal{F}(U),
2846	  \]
2847	  natural in \(\mathcal{F}\). Thus \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the global-sections
2848	  functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
2849	\end{lemma}
2850	
2851	\begin{proof}
2852	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2853	  Compose two natural additive isomorphisms. The sheafification adjunction
2854	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) of
2855	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction} transports corepresentability through
2856	  sheafification:
2857	  \[
2858	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(
2859	      \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)),\, \mathcal{F}
2860	    \bigr)
2861	    \;\cong\;
2862	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2863	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr).
2864	  \]
2865	  The free–Yoneda additive equivalence \(\operatorname{freeYonedaHomAddEquiv}\) of
2866	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} then identifies the right-hand
2867	  presheaf Hom-group with the evaluation \((\iota\mathcal{F})(U) = \mathcal{F}(U) =
2868	  \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\):
2869	  \[
2870	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2871	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr)
2872	    \;\cong\;
2873	    \mathcal{F}(U).
2874	  \]
2875	  Both isomorphisms are additive and natural in \(\mathcal{F}\), so their composite is
2876	  the asserted natural additive isomorphism.
2877	\end{proof}
2878	
2879	\begin{definition}
2880	
2881	\leanok
2882	[Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object]
2883	  \label{def:absolute_cohomology}
2884	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomology}
2885	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:jshriek_corepr, lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib,
2886	    lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib, lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib,
2887	    lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib, lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}
2888	  \textit{Realization of absolute sheaf cohomology in the module-sheaf category.}
2889	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(U \subseteq
2890	  X\) an open subscheme with open immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\). Let
2891	  \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U \in X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be the corepresenting object of
2892	  Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}. The \emph{absolute sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2893	  over \(U\)} is defined to be the Ext of this corepresenting object in the single
2894	  abelian category \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
2895	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:
2896	  \[
2897	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\; \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}
2898	      \bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\; \mathcal{F}\bigr),
2899	    \qquad p \geq 0.
2900	  \]
2901	  Both Ext arguments live in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); no restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2902	  along \(j\) is taken. Here \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is Mathlib's Ext bifunctor
2903	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); the
2904	  \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure it requires is available unconditionally by
2905	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}. The three structural facts the downstream
2906	  dimension-shift arguments consume are then exactly the corresponding Mathlib results:
2907	  \begin{enumerate}
2908	  \item \textbf{Degree zero is global sections.} In degree \(0\), Ext is the Hom-group,
2909	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2910	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F})\), by the
2911	    degree-zero Ext comparison (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}); composing
2912	    with the corepresentability isomorphism
2913	    \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2914	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} gives \(H^0(U,
2915	    \mathcal{F}) \cong \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
2916	  \item \textbf{Injective vanishing.} When the \emph{second} Ext argument is an
2917	    injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\), one has
2918	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(-, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) by
2919	    Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}. Since \(\mathcal{I}\) is exactly
2920	    the second argument of \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{I})\),
2921	    this yields \(H^{n+1}(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) directly: the short exact sequence stays
2922	    in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\), no restriction is taken, and
2923	    restriction-preserves-injectives is not needed.
2924	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence.} For a short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F}
2925	    \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) and the
2926	    \emph{fixed} first argument \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), the covariant Ext long exact
2927	    sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) is the long exact sequence of
2928	    the groups \(H^p(U, -)\), with connecting maps given by composition with the Ext
2929	    class of the extension.
2930	  \end{enumerate}
2931	\end{definition}
2932	
2933	\begin{definition}
2934	[Ext bifunctor in an abelian category]
2935	  \label{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}
2936	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext}
2937	  \mathlibok
2938	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2939	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
2940	  For an abelian category \(C\) with a \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure and objects \(X, Y
2941	  \in C\) and \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), Mathlib provides the Ext group
2942	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^n_C(X, Y)\), functorial (contravariantly in \(X\), covariantly in
2943	  \(Y\)) and equipped with a composition pairing.
2944	\end{definition}
2945	
2946	\begin{lemma}
2947	[Unconditional availability of \(\mathrm{HasExt}\)]
2948	  \label{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}
2949	  \lean{CategoryTheory.HasExt.standard}
2950	  \mathlibok
2951	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2952	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
2953	  Every abelian category \(C\) carries a \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure; in particular the
2954	  module-sheaf category \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\) admits the Ext bifunctor of
2955	  Definition~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib} unconditionally.
2956	\end{lemma}
2957	
2958	\begin{lemma}
2959	[Degree-zero Ext is the Hom-group]
2960	  \label{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}
2961	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.homEquiv₀,
2962	    CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀}
2963	  \mathlibok
2964	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2965	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
2966	  For objects \(A, Y\) of an abelian category with \(\mathrm{HasExt}\), there is a
2967	  natural (additive) isomorphism \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(A, Y) \cong (A \to Y)\) between
2968	  degree-zero Ext and the Hom-group. Applied to the first argument \(A =
2969	  j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), this identifies \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2970	  \mathcal{F})\) with
2971	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F})\), which the
2972	  corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} in turn identifies
2973	  with \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
2974	\end{lemma}
2975	
2976	\begin{lemma}
2977	[Ext vanishes on injectives in positive degree]
2978	  \label{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}
2979	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.eq_zero_of_injective}
2980	  \mathlibok
2981	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib

3194	The Serre vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} is obtained by feeding a concrete
3195	\emph{affine} cover system into the basis-comparison criterion. We construct that cover system
3196	(Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system}) and discharge its three nontrivial fields, then supply the
3197	quasi-coherent seed that meets condition (3). The sub-lemmas below are the formalize-ready cut points
3198	of this instantiation.

3204	  \label{lem:affine_faces_mem}

3206	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}

3209	  % L513--517).
3210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that $\Spec(R)$ has a basis of open sets $D(f)$,

3212	  % Definition \ref{algebra-definition-Zariski-topology}.
3213	  % In addition, the intersection of two standard opens is another:

3216	  (standard opens).}
3217	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) the associated affine scheme. The
3218	  distinguished opens \(D(f)\), \(f \in R\), form a basis closed under intersection: \(D(f) \cap D(g)
3219	  = D(fg)\). Consequently, for any standard open cover \(\mathcal{U} : V = \bigcup_{i \in \iota}
3220	  D(g_i)\) of a distinguished open \(V\), every finite intersection \(D(g_{i_0}) \cap \cdots \cap
3221	  D(g_{i_p}) = D(g_{i_0} \cdots g_{i_p})\) of its members is again a distinguished open. This is the
3222	  \(\mathrm{faces\_mem}\) field (condition (1)) of the affine cover system.
3223	\end{lemma}
3224	\begin{proof}
3225	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}
3226	  \leanok
3227	  The identity \(D(f) \cap D(g) = D(fg)\) holds because a prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) avoids both \(f\) and
3228	  \(g\) iff it avoids the product \(fg\). Iterating over the indices \(i_0, \ldots, i_p\) of a face
3229	  gives \(\bigcap_k D(g_{i_k}) = D\bigl(\prod_k g_{i_k}\bigr)\), a distinguished open; the base set
3230	  \(V = D(f)\) is distinguished by hypothesis. Hence every face of a standard cover lies in the basis.
3231	\end{proof}

3334	  \emph{Step 2 (refine to a standard cover carrying local lifts).} Local surjectivity at \(t\)
3335	  produces an open covering \(\{W_\alpha\}\) of \(V\) together with sections
3336	  \(t_\alpha \in S_2(W_\alpha)\) mapping to \(t|_{W_\alpha}\). Since the affine opens form a basis
3337	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:scheme_isBasis_affineOpens}) and standard covers are cofinal among open coverings
3338	  of \(V\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:standard_cover_cofinal}), refine \(\{W_\alpha\}\) to a standard cover
3339	  \(\mathcal{U} = \{D(g_i)\} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) of \(V\); restricting the \(t_\alpha\) along the
3340	  refinement yields local lifts \(s^{\mathrm{loc}}_i \in S_2(D(g_i))\) with
3341	  \(g(s^{\mathrm{loc}}_i) = t|_{D(g_i)}\).
3342	
3343	  \emph{Step 3 (glue via \(\check{H}^1 = 0\)).} The left term \(S_1\) has vanishing positive
3344	  {\v C}ech cohomology over every covering of \(V\) in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) (the field hypothesis); in
3345	  particular \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, S_1) = 0\). Feeding the cover \(\mathcal{U}\), the local lifts
3346	  \(s^{\mathrm{loc}}\), and this vanishing to the {\v C}ech \(\check{H}^1\)-surjectivity criterion
3347	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1}, which is stated over a raw finite family of opens) produces a global
3348	  section \(s \in S_2(V)\) with \(g(s) = t\). Hence \(S_2(V) \to S_3(V)\) is surjective.
3349	\end{proof}

3351	\begin{lemma}
3352	[The underlying-abelian-sheaf functor preserves epimorphisms]
3353	  \label{lem:to_sheaf_preserves_epi}
3354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.toSheaf_preservesEpimorphisms}
3355	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system}
3356	  \textit{Project-bespoke gap-fill (instance).}
3357	  The forgetful functor from sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to the underlying sheaves of
3358	  abelian groups preserves epimorphisms: if \(g : S_2 \to S_3\) is an epimorphism of
3359	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules then the induced morphism of underlying abelian sheaves is an
3360	  epimorphism. Indeed the forgetful functor is exact (it has both adjoints and reflects the relevant
3361	  colimits), so it carries the cokernel-zero characterisation of an epimorphism to the same
3362	  characterisation downstairs. This is the one small instance unlocking the passage from a module
3363	  epimorphism to local surjectivity in Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_surj_of_vanishing}.
3364	\end{lemma}
3365	\begin{proof}
3366	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system}
3367	  An epimorphism in an abelian category is characterised by vanishing cokernel. The underlying
3368	  abelian-sheaf functor on sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is additive and exact (it preserves
3369	  finite colimits, being a left adjoint composed with the exact sheafification–forgetful comparison),
3370	  so it sends the cokernel of \(g\) to the cokernel of the image of \(g\); since the former is zero,
3371	  so is the latter, and the image of \(g\) is an epimorphism.
3372	\end{proof}

3374	\begin{lemma}[Locally surjective morphisms of presheaves]
3375	  \label{lem:presheaf_is_locally_surjective}
3376	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective}
3377	  \mathlibok
3378	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3379	  For a Grothendieck topology \(J\), a morphism \(\alpha : \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}\) of presheaves
3380	  is \emph{locally surjective} when every section of \(\mathcal{H}\) over an object is, locally on a
3381	  covering sieve, in the image of \(\alpha\). On a topological space this is the predicate
3382	  \(\operatorname{TopCat.Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective}\): for each open \(V\) and each section
3383	  \(t \in \mathcal{H}(V)\) there is an open covering \(\{W_\alpha\}\) of \(V\) with \(t|_{W_\alpha}\)
3384	  in the image of \(\alpha_{W_\alpha}\).
3385	\end{lemma}

3387	\begin{lemma}[An epimorphism of sheaves is locally surjective]
3388	  \label{lem:sheaf_locally_surjective_iff_epi}
3389	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.isLocallySurjective_iff_epi'}
3390	  \mathlibok

3525	  % statement. The unconditional upgrade awaits the 01I8 affine global-generation instance;

3527	  % for the presentation-driven discharge.
